comments.title: Hekate
comments.content:
I have a Venus talisman and somewhat formally asked Venus on her hour on a Friday to start working together regularly. What came through pretty clearly was Hekate would be the one to work with which triggered a memory of an astrological chart reading I had done a few years ago. The analysis indicated Venus in Scorpio in the 9th house (house of God, spiritual teachers) and “Hekate, Kali, Shakti, LHP, bhakti yoga, path of devotion”.

I am generally in alignment with what you write on occultism. My reaction to people working with the spirits of the Goetia and the associated risks has always been Why? I think I remember you writing something to the same effect that I read later on in my explorations. I love angelic energy, so I’m hesitant (also a bit curious) to work with spirits associated with the underworld. That scorpio energy has always been dealt with by me using music, art etc...

My question is do you have any knowledge of working with Hekate or a deity like this from your own experience or others? How can it turn out in a relationship with this type of spirit? I have no interest in demons, necromancy, chaotic unstable forces and the like, but Hekate seems to represent something in between. How can one work with a planet in detriment? Is planetary charity the only way or could one “go with it” and work with the planet as placed to positive effect? Any insight is appreciated.

replies.title: Re: Hekate
replies.content:
Hekate is far from an entirely malefic deity. Here's what Hesiod wrote about her:

"And she conceived and bare Hecate whom Zeus the son of Cronos honoured above all. He gave her splendid gifts, to have a share of the earth and the unfruitful sea. She received honour also in starry heaven, and is honoured exceedingly by the deathless gods. For to this day, whenever any one of men on earth offers rich sacrifices and prays for favour according to custom, he calls upon Hecate. Great honour comes full easily to him whose prayers the goddess receives favourably, and she bestows wealth upon him; for the power surely is with her. For as many as were born of Earth and Ocean amongst all these she has her due portion. The son of Cronos did her no wrong nor took anything away of all that was her portion among the former Titan gods: but she holds, as the division was at the first from the beginning, privilege both in earth, and in heaven, and in sea. Also, because she is an only child, the goddess receives not less honour, but much more still, for Zeus honours her. Whom she will she greatly aids and advances: she sits by worshipful kings in judgement, and in the assembly whom she will is distinguished among the people. And when men arm themselves for the battle that destroys men, then the goddess is at hand to give victory and grant glory readily to whom she will. Good is she also when men contend at the games, for there too the goddess is with them and profits them: and he who by might and strength gets the victory wins the rich prize easily with joy, and brings glory to his parents. And she is good to stand by horsemen, whom she will: and to those whose business is in the grey discomfortable sea, and who pray to Hecate and the loud-crashing Earth-Shaker, easily the glorious goddess gives great catch, and easily she takes it away as soon as seen, if so she will. She is good in the byre with Hermes to increase the stock. The droves of kine and wide herds of goats and flocks of fleecy sheep, if she will, she increases from a few, or makes many to be less. So, then. albeit her mother's only child, she is honoured amongst all the deathless gods. And the son of Cronos made her a nurse of the young who after that day saw with their eyes the light of all-seeing Dawn. So from the beginning she is a nurse of the young, and these are her honours."

If you want to establish a relationship with her, set up an altar with an image of her -- preferably an ancient one, either a replica of a statue or a picture -- burn some incense to her, pray, and then listen inwardly. As for planetary afflictions, those matter in magic but not in religion -- you can pray to the deity of a planet afflicted in your natal chart with good results.

comments.title: Sinister entity investigation, continued
comments.content:
Hi JMG and others,

Happy new year! This is a follow-up to a question I asked last month: https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/257875.html?thread=45185875#cmt45185875

To summarize: I did banishing rituals at the home of some friends and it seems this attracted attention from some unpleasant entities that attacked me in ways that were jarring but ineffectual. One person in the residents' orbit has some disorganized chaos magic-type occult leanings.

Since I posted that I thought about it a bit more and connected some dots. This will be a long post so if it's too much to read don't worry about it, but I wanted to get all the relevant information written down. The chaotic dabbler - let's call her M - set up an altar at one part of the house and seems to mainly be into making bone charms and spell jars. She probably has me pegged for a total "mundane" and I never talk about anything occult with people in this social circle. I've read a good deal on occultism but my practices are still mostly limited to cleansing, meditation and divination.

Now to go into some more detail about that disturbing vision I had after I started banishing at the premises. I had laid down and was in the process of falling asleep when I had a vision that I was standing in a main area of the house, outside the bedroom. I saw what looked like a brownish tabby cat walk in from a door - a door leading to the part of the house where M was at the time. The cat turned, stood and looked at me and I took a closer look at it. As I looked closer, I realized its appearance was completely different than what I perceived at a glance. The face was like a mask with eyeholes instead of eyes and I thought I saw eyes looking at me through the eyeholes, like it was a person wearing the mask. The legs looked like sleeves over a person's limbs but they still bent like a cat's, not a human's, and then I noticed that the cat appeared to be holding a small gray kitten in its mouth, hanging limply by the scruff of its neck.

Was the mouth a cat's mouth or a human's? I couldn't really tell, the visuals were kind of surreal and inconsistent, the kind of thing you can't recall clearly. The kitten looked like it could have been a stuffed doll or a corpse, its visual was kind of abstract and it didn't move. What I remember was that at first the cat-thing looked innocuous, but as I looked at it more closely and registered its costume-like appearance and especially the kitten figure in the mouth, I was struck with an intense sense of malevolence and wrongness. It was at this time that I was suddenly awakened by the feeling of a slap hitting the side of my lower back from behind (I was sleeping on my side). I'm quite sure that I was hit with actual physical force, since I felt the blanket lift up slightly and then settle back down at the place where I was struck. Nowhere near enough force to actually hurt me, though. I was still partially asleep and in a state of paralysis so I summoned all my willpower to break out of the sleeping state and rolled over to see what had hit me. Of course, I saw just the empty room. If someone were to have snuck in and did it they would had to make quite a bit of noise getting in and out, which I didn't hear. What I did hear while in this groggy state was the a curious sort of mewling and growling, like a mutant cat would make, kind of layered over the ambient house sounds. This dissipated as I returned to normal waking awareness.

I was thinking about this again and it hit me. M likes to compare herself to a kitten, including greeting people by saying "meow." She's profoundly two-faced and often talks to people in an exaggerated, saccharine-cutesy way, like a cartoon character. I'm kind of amazed people can stand this and that it doesn't occur to them that this is a blatant mask over her real personality. I also know that she's deeply self-loathing and can be abusive toward people she sees as having less social capital than she does, while toadying up to those with more.

Then I thought of something else. When I stayed at the house, M was over often with her current boyfriend. It doesn't seem like she can sustain relationships very long, big surprise there. The boyfriend, who I'll call D, was someone I'd met and talked to a few times before. Here's the thing. When I saw him doing day-to-day stuff at the house, I didn't recognize him. It felt like I was looking at another person, and he didn't say hi or interact with me or anyone else very much, the only time he left M's side was to get a snack or use the bathroom and I got the impression he was kind of sleepwalking through it. I only witnessed M talk to him once; she asked him a question a couple times and then he gave kind of a slow, halting response. Then there was a party at the house and his personality was totally different - he was talking to people glibly and acting like his old self. I so disoriented by this that I thought that this was yet a third person, separate from M's boyfriend and the D who I remember, and I called him by the wrong name until he corrected me, which was embarassing. But how strange was it that he'd gone through some change that caused me not to recognize him before?

Also, some of the people in the house have had persistent illnesses and slow-healing injuries that suddenly become much better when they spend time away, then revert to normal when they stay at the house again. For example, one resident who has had a persistent foot injury went on a vacation that involved significant amounts of hiking and had no trouble from the foot -- until he came back and the problems returned. Notably, he was nowhere near M during the trip.

Simply put, the creature I envisioned appears to be some kind of avatar for M's personality. Something wearing a flimsy cat-like disguise. The kitten in the mouth could represent parasitism and control of another being. The strange behavior and aspect of the boyfriend made me think of the kitten. Or the kitten could represent M herself in the jaws of something feeding off of her. For someone who hates herself and feels a need to be with someone, I could see her occult focus being on snaring people like D and bending them to her will. She had another boyfriend in the past who was extremely emotionally unbalanced and constantly getting into fights with people but whose mental state would improve by leaps and bounds whenever he was apart from her for even a few days. It was almost as if she was filling him with her subconscious anger which boiled over and led him into conflict with others.

With this in mind, I think the cat-thing could be 1) something M called up for the purpose of manipulating people, or 2) an entity that was lured by some practice of hers and is now feeding off of her and people in the vicinity. Do you think that case 1 or 2 is more likely? In other words, do you think this is something she's calling on consciously or that it's been drawn to her without her direct awareness?

Also, is the way this thing functions consistent with a demon in your view? Judging from your answer to my last post, it sounds like only particularly strong entities will lash out at people for banishing, most are immediately driven away. I would expect a demon to pack a bit more punch than this creature did. After the slap incident and the subsequent nightmares (described in the post above) I simply doubled up the banishing rituals, doing them both before and after sleep and haven't been troubled again.

Finally, can you recommend anything to mitigate this? I suspect your main suggestion would be "split from these people and don't look back" but I have business ties to some of them and like them besides. If I could convince them to stop associating with M I would do so in a heartbeat but she's clearly quite talented at manipulating people who can't see her true nature. She even once did something really awful that caused a falling-out within the group but the people still in the main orbit are just as welcoming and friendly to her as ever. I could try persuading the residents of the house to join in a big cleansing ritual but my intuition tells me not to broach occult topics with them. I think they see spirituality as "just fun and games" and may have a range of bad reactions to my suggestion that they are in some kind of preternatural danger. There's one resident I'm particularly close to who I could perhaps ask some probing questions, ask if anyone's having dreams about a cat, stuff like that, without giving the whole thing away.

I can protect myself by banishing but M clearly isn't good for anyone around her. And for all I know she could have full knowledge that I'm doing something at the house that disrupts her vibe and she may have set the cat-thing on me deliberately. I've judged her a petty dabbler based on what I've heard her say about the occult and the fact that what I can perceive of her aura is weak and incoherent but I may be misjudging her. I would never speak to her about it and I avoid all contact with her that's not absolutely necessary. If she does know, she's never confronted me in a way that suggests it.

replies.title: Re: Sinister entity investigation, continued
replies.content: Since I'm not in a position to investigate this business myself, I'm not going to speculate about what kind of being might be involved in it -- there are many, many kinds of entity out there. The one thing that needs to be said, though, is that it's never a good idea to get caught up in a rescue-game scenario where you're trying to save people from a danger they don't want to be saved from. Pray for them if you have a good relationship with a deity, maintain your own magical practices, and let others choose their own path, however misguided that seems to you.

replies.title: Re: Sinister entity investigation, continued
replies.content: Fair enough, thanks. I'll focus on minimizing my ties to the whole situation.

comments.content: For both JMG and the commentariat: do you know much about Eckhart Tolle and his books? replies.title: Re: Eckhart Tolle
replies.content:
He seems to me to offer a somewhat bland, drily intellectual perspective on enlightenment. As far as I know he comes from no lineage of teaching or study, only his own personal discoveries.

Some people really, really love his approach. It seems some big fans have no awareness that similar topics have been discussed by many other people, for a lot of years now.

I think there's nothing unique about what he teaches, other than the way he puts things connects strongly with certain readers.

Perhaps you could flip through his books at the thrift store. If you like a few pages here and there, you'll like the whole thing. His style is very consistent. If you don't like a few pages here and there, the rest probably won't get any better.

If you aren't truly engaged by his writing, no worries. Many other people can provide similar ideas in different wording.

Mocha Amphibious Moose

comments.title: Chapter 11 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Turning Realizations into Behavior
comments.content:
Good Evening,

For anyone who wasn't around last week, a very happy New Year! For everyone, I hope that you

To Share: For my first blog post of the year, I write up the second-to-last chapter of Kvilhaug's The Seed of Yggdrasill, which is one of the best in the book for making its point in a short and sweet way: https://jpowellrussell.com/#book_the_seed_of_yggdrasill_11_the_trials_of_thorr

To Ask: For topics that get clarified in journaling or meditation (something like "oh wow, I do attach too much importance to attention from others" or "I do get too angry about that sort of thing"), any tips on how to take those realizations from intellectual knowledge and turn them into behavioral and emotional changes (continuing the examples, finding ways to care less about attention and do less to seek it out or not to get as angry about whatever thing you know you get too worked up about)? I know that part of the recipe is "now that you know, make an effort to be different," but any other advice from you, JMG, or the commentariat would be welcome.

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Chapter 11 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Turning Realizations into Behavior
replies.content:
Happy New Year, Jeff. I hope that you too.

The great big discovery of psychology is that what we think is a response to a situation, is often more likely our response to a mental interpretation inside ourselves. A response to a mental perception inside ourselves, not to an instant and perfect mirror showing us the exact truth outside of us. All of these layers of indirection are based on rules, like "if this then at," or "whenever these than also those," that we picked up at some point.

We took those rules in, because believing them reduced an uncomfortable feeling of uncertainty. But they are not necessarily true now. They also were not necessarily true then.

Many rules were conditioned responses to what parents, society, or others trained us to believe, perhaps based on their own subconscious conditioning in their own lives.

Many rules are what we guessed as children would explain our experiences, with all the limitations of a child's perspective.

Many rules are the first idea that happened to come to mind as less painful than having to admit, I have no idea what this really has to mean to me.

Many rules are from the assumption that we have an obligation to reach a conclusion and judgment about everything present in our life. (Social media is especially insidious promoting this.)

Journaling or discursive meditation helps us slow down and observe these connections.

The point of insight comes when we can say, oh! Now I see why I jumped to those predetermined, conditioned reactions. That jump is INSIDE of me, despite unsettling experiences from outside.

Now that I see how that happens, I don't need to make that same jump to conclusions again inside me. Instead, I can pick a different response.

Perhaps I can let the thought pass on by, with no need to hang on to it and explore it.

Perhaps I can let it be its own thing, no longer dragging along Jacob Marley's chains of painful associations from a lifetime of upsets and hurt.

Perhaps I can ask myself, is it really proven that this therefore those and also that, or is that nothing more than a collection of never tested suppositions in my own imagination?

What would be a spiritual counterpoint to this assumption? Would my life be better if I chose the counterpoint this time? If I unhooked myself from both horns of this apparent dilemma?

Does this help?
Christopher from California

replies.title: Re: Chapter 11 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Turning Realizations into Behavior
replies.content: Thanks very much for this, the expanded list of questions for querying such beliefs is certainly helpful.

replies.title: Re: Chapter 11 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Turning Realizations into Behavior
replies.content:
First, work on one at a time. Clarity of focus is an essential tool of magic! Second, define the specific change you want to make in your behavior in reasonable terms. Third, review your actions at the end of each day; don't beat yourself up if you failed or preen yourself if you succeeded, just remember clearly how things went. Fourth, when you've gotten good results on one such project, go on to another, remembering that improvement rather than perfection is the goal.

Awareness is the universal solvent of the alchemists; we stay stuck in habits of thought and action purely because we drift through life half awake so much of the time. Waking up fully solves all such problems.

replies.title: Re: Chapter 11 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Turning Realizations into Behavior
replies.content:
Thank you, Jeff!

Do you by any chance own a time machine, that gives you a 48-hour day? How the heck do you get all this stuff done?

—Princess Cutekitten

replies.title: Re: Chapter 11 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Turning Realizations into Behavior
replies.content:
Hah, I wish! The short answer is "not very well, and not as much as I'd like."

The longer answer, though, is whatever I am able to get done I put down mostly to three things: 1) The kind of stuff I post about here is the kind of stuff I'm strongly intrinsically motivated to do - I really like reading and sharing my thoughts about books, I've found spiritual practice incredibly rewarding, and so forth. 2) I am lucky enough to have a full-time non-research, teaching-only position at a university. That gives me a lot of flexibility in how I use my time outside of class and grading. 3) Back when I was a consultant, before I found the good stuff in the form of real spiritual practice, I was very into self-improvement, and especially productivity, books, podcasts, and so forth, so I picked up a lot of techniques on how to get things done efficiently. Probably the most helpful thing that I've retained better than others is a pretty strong commitment to "single-tasking" - as much as possible, I try to do one thing at a time, and not switch away from it until I'm at some kind of natural stopping point, and the more important the thing, the more focus and time I try to give it. I don't always succeed, of course, but 20 minutes of working on one thing non-stop is waaaay more productive than jumping from one thing to another (at least when it comes to "knowledge work" that involves reading, thinking, and writing).

If you find one of those machines, though, let me know, I could use some more time for sleep!
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Chapter 11 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Turning Realizations into Behavior
replies.content:
any tips on how to take those realizations from intellectual knowledge and turn them into behavioral and emotional changes (continuing the examples, finding ways to care less about attention and do less to seek it out or not to get as angry about whatever thing you know you get too worked up about)?

My mind has less control over my body than I thought. I have only succeeded in suppressing parts of my "self" for a few months at a time.

So instead, when you are visited by your angry self, why not embrace him and get to know him better? For example, when I am visited by my overeating self, I've learned to indulge him. When I do he generally leaves after a few days with more or less manageable health damage. And now that we trust eachother it is possible to trade to the benefit of both.

It is not easy to love your shadow.

replies.title: Re: Chapter 11 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Turning Realizations into Behavior
replies.content:
Thanks very much for this! I've heard of approaches like this in the past, and have had some success trying variations of it, but sometimes I have trouble finding the line between "embrace the shadow so it doesn't gain power from being repressed" and "using 'embrace your shadow' as an excuse to maintain bad habits." The idea of building trust with that part of yourself sounds like a helpful way of approaching it, though, so thank you again.

Jeff

replies.title: Re: Chapter 11 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Turning Realizations into Behavior
replies.content:
Jeff, I'd like to add a bit to the good advice already posted here. The traits you mention are, from the perspective of Heathen soul lore as I propound it, characteristics of the Mod and / or Hugr souls. The Study Guides I have posted on my website, HeathenSoulLore.net, include several that are focused on these souls and the kinds of issues that you bring up here. In particular there are Study Guide 7 "Mod and Hugr as Motivating Forces," and Study Guide 10 "Will and Wish: The Dynamism of Mod and Hugr." (As well as SG 8 and 9 on these souls individually.)

The idea is to recognize these and many similar traits as aspects of soul-beings who all together make up who we are, and to realize that our surface-mind and its motivations and perspectives are not the be-all and end-all of who we are. These study guides suggest that we objectively and respectfully observe these soul-beings within us, seeking first to know and understand them as we would another human separate from us, without immediately seeking to criticize, control, or suppress them.

I refer to all our souls together as our 'soular system' or soul-household, and just as in a household of individual people, when there is friction and disagreement the first thing to do is seek an understanding of the people involved and where they are coming from. As JMG put it so well, awareness is the universal solvent. By recognizing that our souls are individual beings it reminds us that they are due the same respect that we give to other people, rather than believing it is alright to beat up on ourselves when our inner selves or souls disagree with each other about what we want or what is the 'right' behavior.

There's a lot that could be said about the specific examples you gave, Jeff, from a Heathen perspective. For example, 'seeking attention' can, in its mature form, morph into the Heathen desire to make a great name for oneself, one's 'gefrain' or reputation, not by "demanding attention" but by the quality of the deeds, great and small, that we engage in and the way we live our lives to the highest of our own standards and ideals. Beowulf is, perhaps, the best-known exemplar of this. From what little I know of you, it seems to me you are well along on this path, yourself.

The praise and acknowledgement most importantly first comes from our own self, our own souls, well-earned by our deeds, and perhaps you deserve to get more of this from your own self / souls? Once that essential need is satisfied, once our Mod and Hugr souls can stand tall in their own well-earned pride, we become less needy for the attention from others, though that continues to be important to us too.

When we recognize our holistic self as a group of (often rather difficult!) individual souls, we can provide a lot of what we seek from other people by our own interactions among our souls. We still need and want other people, always, but the nature of our needs changes when we can satisfy some of those needs from within ourselves. This, in turn, helps us pursue less needy, more well-balanced, mature, and satisfying relationships with other people. It all begins by getting to know our own souls, honestly and respectfully, and I offer exercises for pursuing this in all the Study Guides on Mod and Hugr.

Jeff, thank you for your review of Chapter 11 of Seed of Yggdrasill; I read it through and found it very helpful!

Best wishes to all, and thanks to JMG for these Magic Mondays!

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: Chapter 11 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Turning Realizations into Behavior
replies.content:
Winifred, thank you as always for your kind words and thoughtful response! I think the soul lore that you teach may well have some useful paths to dealing with these kinds of things (though, to be clear, the examples were meant as "typical," and while close to some of the things I want to deal with, I didn't want to lay everything out there in the open). Specifically, having some idea of what each soul wants/needs/is "for" seems like a helpful extra piece of information that only thinking about a "subconscious" or even a "shadow" within the subconscious doesn't necessarily do.

Despite Princess Cutekitten's response above, unluckily, I've not yet had time to dig into either your Soul Lore books or much into the study guides on your site, due to the other spiritual work I've been doing, but maybe I will take a look and see if there are any lower-intensity ways I might integrate some of the work, like using it as an aid to journaling and the like.

Thanks again!
Jeff

We got a couple of inches of snow, then rain came and melted most of it away, and then the snow came back and laid down another inch or so. It's expected to rain again Tuesday, so the snow won't be here for long. No Snow-Cats needed in sunny Providence!

As for the Northeast, it's not at all a single place; each state has its own distinct culture and history. We lived in far western Maryland for nine years, up in the Appalachians, before Sara's health problems and some other factors made it necessary to head for someplace less rural and isolated; otherwise we'd have stayed put as it's very pleasant and the cost of living is astonishingly low. Rhode Island is also pleasant; East Providence, where we live, is a quiet working class town where Portuguese is the second language. Providence, across the Seekonk River, is green, quiet, understated, with excellent parks and a very good public transit system; it reminds me a lot of what Seattle was like forty years ago, before the software industry and unchecked development turned it into smog, subdivisions, and strip malls from Puget Sound straight up into the mountains.

No, we didn't move here for the sake of my writing; I can write anywhere. We came here because I had a speaking gig, decided we liked it, found that it had all the things we needed to get out of Cumberland MD to get, and moved.

comments.title: 5 Rites and Baguazhang
comments.content:
Hey JMG,
First of all, a big thanks to you for writing The Secret of the Five Rites! Phenomenal job (as always). I've been working the program you laid out for the last few weeks, and am experiencing noticible results! I had attempted the Rites over 25 years ago, but lost interest quickly due to lack of knowledge of the "why" of the exercises. Your instruction on the internal alchemical workings of the system convinced me to immediately insert the Rites into my daily practice. Which leads me to, not so much a question, but a possible connection to another art that I practice, that I would like to get your opinion on.
For over 25 years now, I've practiced and taught the art of Baguazhang. For those who don't know, Baguazhang is a sister art of Taichi. An internal martial art, it is unique in that it is based primarily on Daoist circle walking meditation, where one walks in a circle, holding different postures. As I was taught, it focuses on bringing up the telluric current, and drawing down the solar current into the body, channeling it via the postures to different centers of the body for Alchemical purposes.
It was first taught by Dong Hai Chuan in the late 1880s in Beijing. His 1st student was Yin Fu, who learned the complete Bagua system, which includes not only the martial methods, but meditation, divination, internal alchemy, and a system of medicine.
Yin Fu taught Men Baozhen, who taught the complete system to Dr. Xie Peiqi. Dr Xie broke with tradition and taught openly so the system wouldn't die out. Andrew Nugent-Head began to document the entire system, which is where I learned it, as well as learning from He Jinbao, Dr. Xie's top martial student.
Anyway, I was taught the Yin Bagua Morning Routine, which is a dao yin/yoga routine practiced to the four directions. And here's the funny thing. The routine contains four of the five rites as well as variations of the rites! The only rite it doesn't contain is the 1st rite of spinning. But that need could be taken care of in the system with the Circle Walking practice.
I'm not positive how the Rites ended up in the morning routine of martial artists in 1880-1930 Beijing, but maybe not so coincidentally, it just so happens that during the years between WWI and WWII, W.E. Fairbairn, a British man, was Captain of the Shanghai police. He became a student of Cui Zhendong, a top Bagua student of Yin Fu. Through his work, he developed a system of hand-to-hand combat, which he brought back to England. He subsequently taught the special forces of both the UK and US what would come to be known as WWII combatives, also a passion of mine.
What I find fascinating is the connection between the West and China at this time, and the fact that basically all five of the Rites ended up in a Daoist internal martial art which seemingly had no connection to the occult scene of the West in the early 1900s.
Thanks,
Geoff

replies.title: Re: 5 Rites and Baguazhang
replies.content:
That's utterly fascinating. I'm familiar with Baguazhang, though I haven't practiced more than a few preparatory exercises for it; I spent ten years studying taijiquan with Andrew Dale in Seattle and then his student Gene Burnett in Ashland OR, and Andy taught the basics of baguazhang and xingyiquan to all his taijiquan students.

There was far more interaction between Asian and Western exercise systems in the early 20th century than most people are aware; martial artists in China and yogis in India were well aware of what was going on in the American and European physical culture scene, not least because books in Western languages were readily available in the larger Asian cities.

Can you recommend a published source for the Yin Bagua Morning Routine? If there's ever an updated edition I'd like to be able to cite something.

replies.title: Re: 5 Rites and Baguazhang
replies.content:
Thanks for the reply! I'm not sure if the morning routine was ever documented in print. There is a teaching seminar that Andrew recorded, wherein he also teaches a lot of the other Yin style bagua fascia/sinew channel training:
https://association-for-traditional-studies.teachable.com/courses/category/internal-cultivation
There is an official book by Men Baozhen, which may contain it, but it was never translated, only a few copies survived after the Cultural Revolution, and my grasp of Chinese is rudimentary at best anyway. I'll keep digging, and if I find anything else, I'll let ya know!

replies.title: Re: 5 Rites and Baguazhang
replies.content: Thank you! One of the reasons I published The Secret of the Five Rites was precisely the hope that other scraps of data would surface -- and clearly one just has.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Okay, my experience with sign talismans is that they need to be handled somewhat differently. I got the best results by (a) making sure the ruler of the sign was well dignified both essentially and accidentally, (b) making sure a well dignified benefic is in the sign, and (c) putting either the sign or its ruler on the ascendant or midheaven. I like to have the waxing Moon applying to the sign ruler by trine or sextile; if you're doing a Scorpio talisman, in other words, and Mars is in Capricorn, it's best to have the Moon in Taurus (since she's exalted there) trine Mars, but you can also have her waxing in Virgo or Pisces. (Not in Scorpio, since that's the sign of her fall and she's therefore ill dignified.)

2) You have to learn a completely different system of magic. As Agrippa points out, mathematical/astrological magic, the subject of his Second Book, is a different kettle of fish from spiritual/supercelestial magic, the subject of his Third Book. The methods I use for talismans of supercelestial forces are Golden Dawn rituals, which invoke the sephiroth rather than the planets and do so in ways suitable for supercelestial forces not influenced by time or space.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) You'll have to ask him. Notice that my response talked about my experience; his may be different, and so may yours.

2) Crowley was wrong. The Golden Dawn system is very powerful but there are many kinds of magic it won't do. You can conjure Barakhiel using Golden Dawn methods, sure, but you do that by first uniting yourself in prayer and contemplation with ALHIM GBVR, then calling on the relevant archangel and descending the planes from there.

The fluid condenser, not the paper, then holds the charge.

comments.title: Ecosophia Prayer List
comments.content:
The Ecosophia Prayer List celebrated its one year anniversay on December 22nd! In the last year, there were many more prayer requests than I originally anticipated, but I'm happy for it. Several people reported serious illness recovering faster than doctors expected, including cases which weren't expected to turn around at all.

One piece of feedback that I've gotten is that some people are overwhelmed by the sheer number of prayer entries. This is completely understandable; there are indeed many, and is a bit daunting even to me. I think some new tactics are warranted to manage the list.

First of all, as I've been warning I would do for some weeks, I've removed all old prayers older than 6 months (with a couple of exceptions that my intuition encouraged me to keep). Next, I am revising the policy regarding how long entries remain on the list:

  • Except where specifically requested otherwise, all prayers for the dead will come off of the list after a month and a day. 
  • The same goes for prayer requests for pets: a month and a day, unless specifically requested otherwise. 
  • All other prayers can remain for 3 months without an update (though updates are appreciated). This will be a harder line than I allowed before. I reserve the discretion to allow certain prayers for longer, when serious medical issues or the like are involved.

As before, updates on how things are going with what you've made a request about are always appreciated. Every time you do, if your prayer is not yet resolved, the 3 month clock starts over.

As I've changed the terms, all entries that are currently remaining on the list are grandfathered in, and safe from such removal until the Spring Equinox.

May the coming year be filled with blessings for all!

* * *

This week I would like to bring special attention to the following prayer requests.

May the surgery for Yuccaglauca's mother Monica's malignant mass be safe, successful, and conclusive of the matter.

May Frank Rudolf Hartman of Altadena California (picture), who is receiving chemotherapy, be completely cured of the lymphoma that is afflicting him, and may he return to full health. 

May the brain surgery that Erika's partner James underwent for his cancer on October 16th have gone successfully; and may he be blessed, healed and protected, and successfully treated for all of his cancer.

May Kyle's friend Amanda, who though in her early thirties is undergoing various difficult treatments for brain cancer, make a full recovery; and may her body and spirit heal with grace.

Lp9's hometown, East Palestine, Ohio, for the safety and welfare of their people, animals and all living beings in and around East Palestine, and to improve the natural environment there to the benefit of all.
 
* * *

Old guidelines for how long prayer requests stay on the list, how to word requests, how to be added to the weekly email list, how to improve the chances of your prayer being answered, and several other common questions and issues, are to be found at the Ecosophia Prayer List FAQ. (It does not yet accord with the new policies I've listed above; I'll update it soon.)

If there are any among you who might wish to join me in a bit of astrological timing, I pray each week for the health of all those with health problems on the list on the astrological hour of the Sun on Sundays, bearing in mind the Sun's rulerships of heart, brain, and vital energies. If this appeals to you, I invite you to join me.

replies.title: Re: Ecosophia Prayer List
replies.content: Thank you for this as always.

replies.title: Re: Ecosophia Prayer List
replies.content:
I thought I'd chime in and say that one thing I've done to try to cope with the large number of prayers is that during my daily prayer, I light a candle to "All of the Gods, Goddesses and Well Wights (Good Spirits, in an inclusive sense of "spirits") who have answered and will answer prayers for the Ecosophia community prayer list." comments.title: Qs
comments.content:
Greetings!

1) With people such as John Neumann and other geniuses, is it the case that souls greatly develop (almost overdevelop) their mental capacities but don't move on to spiritual work and thus hang around longer over multiple lives?

Steiner said something about materialist souls such Francis bacon reincarnating, for example.

2) Are emotions actually in the body or is that just a correspondence between a body part and the brain where the emotions actually? Some time of attention pattern? (such as when they say in chinese medicine that fear is in the kidneys).

If emotions are in the astral (or etheric) bodies, are they all over or do they correspond to the same area as the brain?

3) I am trying to respond to some materialist buddhists who argue that energy bodies aren't real but are some type of attention pattern of the body. What would be a good way to think about this topic?

replies.title: Re: Qs
replies.content:
Not JMG but I'll share some thoughts that might help clarify.

2) I believe people use the words feelings and emotions to describe a wide range of experiences.

There are purely physical reactions seeking food or avoiding pain, that we share with amoebas.
There's king of the hill fight or flight we share with lizards, deep in the limbic system.

There are states we share with cats and dogs, such as comfort, love, contentment, eagerness, fear, disgust. There's determined puzzle-solving we share with crows. Much of this animal level of energy flow is well described by traditional Chinese medicine, as in your kidneys example (linked to I Ching 29 The Water Abyss of rapids plunging through the canyon, powerful and dangerous), or the over hyped heart when an excess of joy turns into mania.

Then there's another whole range of physical reactions to metaphysical view in our imagination of times other than the present, like hope, terror, worry, anxiety.
There are body reactions to a conclusion of danger or of opportunity which are interpretations of conceptual ideas about what things mean to us.
Stories, myth, religion, mystical esoteric experience, can lead to astral and physical effort to ground abstractions that come beyond ordinary physical thought.

This isn't a complete list.

I think it's much more empowering to discuss a specific emotional experience and trace it, if we can, to its roots, rather than lump emotions together. For example, to commit to not let childhood pattern of supplicating pleading interfere with adult business talks, or to recognize when anger at a neighbor is from a conceptual threat to self-image, not from physical harm.
These specifics are more empowering, more useful than a vague resolution like "I should be less emotional," "I want to be more emotionally appropriate," "I want to feel emotionally closer to my siblings." A good follow up question: How exactly would that work? What exactly would that look like?

3) Doesn't materialist mean they've already concluded they reject any non-materialistic explanation? If so, is their view like going to the All Black & White Photography Club for advice about red and green?

Christopher from California

replies.title: Re: Qs
replies.content:
1) Unlikely, since a soul in need of spiritual work will be born into situations where it's very hard to avoid doing spiritual work! I'd point out instead that each soul has its own unique capacities and potentials, and the soul that incarnated as John von Neumann may have had a lot of intellectual potential to express.

2) Emotions manifest through the body but their basis is in the astral body. On the astral level, not only are they all over, they extend beyond the physical body into the aura. Here's what one clairvoyant saw, and painted:
[IMAGE:0]

3) Never argue with materialists. Their beliefs are a closed system that functions by reinterpreting everything in terms of matter via ad hoc hypotheses and circular reasoning. "Everything that exists is material. How do we know this? Because nobody has ever experienced anything that isn't material. What about all the people who say otherwise? They're wrong, because everything is material." Around and around they go!

comments.title: Karate and etheric energy
comments.content:
JMG,

I have been reading about some of the early Westerners to study karate and Japanese martial arts in the 1950s and 60s and their memoirs (eg CW Nicol’s Moving Zen etc) and there are some fascinating anecdotes in there before karate got watered down into a sport - for example the author personally saw:

1. Someone karate chopping three bricks piled on top of one another, but using his ki to “target” only the middle brick such that the top and bottom brick stayed intact but only the middle brick broke. Would this have been through some kind of targeted use of etheric energy or clever application of leverage and force transmission? Or possibly both

2. Same question in relation to the same person being able to drive his fingers with power into a wooden post inches deep (I’m really not even sure how this is possible)

3. He says he witnessed a tai chi master from China casually slap the wooden supporting pillar holding up part of his house and making the whole house shake (he had actually woken up thinking it was an earth quake and come downstairs to see his housemates studying with the visitor). Apparently he asked his karate sensei about it and how effective tai chi is (the sensei had also studied in China) and the guy said something like “for most people karate is the best to learn and gives the best results, but there are some super humans who have truly mastered tai chi and get super human results”

replies.title: Re: Karate and etheric energy
replies.content:
The East Asian martial arts have developed the use of the very dense levels of the etheric plane to a high pitch -- more so than anywhere else in the world, as far as I know. All this stuff is done with ki/qi aka etheric substance. It takes many years of intensive training to get to the point of being able to do such things, and a lot of the training is qigong (etheric practices).

I've seen a guy set up a stack of Chinese roof tiles, ask the audience to tell him which one to break, and then slap the top tile and have the one they named explode into fragments while the others remained unharmed. It's pretty spectacular stuff.

comments.title: models of divine beings
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I was wondering what you thought of the energy vs psychological vs spirit models of magic. In the energy model, it seems that everything is the 4 elements and their manipulation. The energy model sounds to me like Eliphas Levi's approach of manipulating the Astral Light. I think he took this approach because he was trying to come up with a scientific rational. Then, there is the psychological model of Israel Regardie where the divine beings are parts of the psyche. Finally, there is the spirit model of traditional pre-Levi magic where the divine beings are real entities that should be treated as such. In the Picatrix and PGM offerings should be given to them to build a relationship. It seems that in the later Solomonic tradition the pagan gods became devils. While in the traditions following Levi, the pagan gods became either names for energy or archetypes of the unconscious.

Which model, if any, do you think is closest to the truth?

replies.title: Re: models of divine beings
replies.content: Models aren't true, they're just useful. In all probability none of these models are anything close to the truth -- they're simply the best that our brains can manage when dealing with a universe that really is incomprehensible.

replies.title: Re: models of divine beings
replies.content: Maybe once the copyright renewal on the universe is filed, it will make more sense. Until then, any old pantheon can make their own derivative spinoffs of the astral realm. And not even need to pay royalties to Sophia's Dad.

replies.title: Re: models of divine beings
replies.content:
These don't seem incompatible? Political scientists and economists talk about aggregate forces as though they were impersonal energies all the time, even when the aggregates are made out of the individual decisions of thinking beings. Stage managers of audience-participation performances (like rock concerts), political strategists, media producers, and domestic propaganda and education departments have to handle forces sometimes as though they were impersonal energies and sometimes as though they're psychological states to be evoked and manipulated. And there's no problem with these qualifying as different practically exposed aspects of a single entirely consistent world. The definition of "closest to the truth" would, in the case of those examples, be mostly relative to what kinds of effects you expected to need to be able to plan around producing. (In another sense, the model "closest to the truth" would be a full causal description of all the individual minds involved, except then in that case it's not clear what it would even mean for a model to be "true" if it can neither be cognitively handled nor represented.)

The supernatural world as it appears to us is plausibly designed partly to provide implicit instruction to something that will become of what's later left of us. If the aspects of the cosmos sometimes seem like impersonal circumstance-independently consistently acting forces, and sometimes seem like personal circumstance-dependent variably acting beings, and sometimes seem like motifs in the mind of an author or a dreamer whose storytelling we can influence by sympathetic operations on our own deeply entangled minds, these might all be reflections of different distilled features of deeper truths about a world that "we" may encounter with greater immediacy later. And that doesn't seem so strange, if such appearances can emerge so readily even within this world.

(Even materiality itself could also be fit into this same proposal, as materiality has some qualities similar to bureaucracy, jurisprudential precedents, and the settlements produced by adversarial legal proceedings or the balancing of interests in legislative lobbying. Though I don't know how elemental spirits might feel about my likening them to narrower-scoped bureaus of a bureaucracy!)

And even within just the paradigm of impersonal "energies" and "elements", there's still clearly some kind of necessary aspect of personal subjectivity or co-construction. The "earth", "water", and "fire" in the Western element system differ substantially from the "earth", "water", and "fire" in the Eastern process-state system, and yet we get effective systems of practice that rely on such concepts both in Western magic and in Eastern alchemy. It seems like there has to be something that intelligently "routes" practitioners' process of inquiry and development, so that if a tradition starts from a vague idea of "earth" and tries to come to terms with a deeper underlying essential principle, in one context it can arrive at some practical relationship with an efficacious Western element, and in another context it can arrive at some practical relationship with an efficacious Eastern process-state. Did those efficacious principles already exist in the eventual particular detailed form that the tradition arrived at? Or were they elicited and shaped over time? Or is the question meaningless because there was a plenum of systems of already existent already efficacious principles, and the question was just one of the tradition narrowing down which variation it was going to connect to? In any of those cases except arguably the last, it seems like there must be some personal intelligence carrying some of the load, other than that of the practitioners themselves. In that sense it would have never been correct to say the "energies" were purely impersonal, such that they could be contacted through interaction just with purely impersonal parts of a magical cosmology.

comments.title: Communicating with the Departed
comments.content:
Hi, JMG,

There was mention made last week of Theosophy and its adversarial relationship with Spiritualism. I came across an article by this Theosophical group:

https://blavatskytheosophy.com/the-danger-and-deception-of-channelling/

One of the teachings of Theosophy is that after death, the individual's "kama rupa" eventually dissolves, and the individual eventually goes into a state where there is no contact with people on the physical plane until it's time to reincarnate.

This is similar to what a Buddhist friend told me the Tibetans teach, although instead of "kama rupa", they use the term "lha". My friend said his Tibetan teacher told him that a departed person's "lha" is usually what mediums are contacting, and not the actual person.

According to these traditions, a lot of what constitutes an individual's personality is contained in the "kama rupa" or "lha."

From the perspective of your traditions, is this correct or a bit off? I guess what I'm asking is how long do we retain memory and the personality of our lives on earth before all that dissolves? In my mind, what would seem to go against this is that I personally know people who have had dead relatives show up many years after the fact and give them information which was later proven to be correct.

replies.title: Re: Communicating with the Departed
replies.content:
From the perspective of the traditions I study and practice, this isn't quite right. There are certainly problems with spiritualism -- notably, you have no way to be sure that the entity who claims to be Aunt Mildred really is Aunt Mildred -- but the memories and personality of a given life don't just dissolve; if that were the case, what would be the point of reincarnation?

No, what happens is that your real self -- your Individuality, to use Dion Fortune's label -- takes on a personality when it enters into incarnation. The personality is assembled from the Individuality's memories of previous lives, with an eye toward what it can glimpse of the life to come and its overall purpose and destiny. Once the soul (Individuality plus personality) incarnates, the personality is then shaped by the events of that life. When the life ends, the personality and all its memories become part of the Individuality, one of the resources it uses to build new personalities. Over time, too, as the Individuality gains experience, its personalities become closer and closer to the Individuality, until finally the two are identical and the person awakens to his or her eternal identity.

Now of course all this contradicts one of the most basic tenets of Buddhism, the claim that there is no eternal identity, no immortal soul...but that's one of the places where I think the Buddhists are quite simply wrong. Make of that what you will.

replies.title: Re: Communicating with the Departed
replies.content:
Some occultists -- I am one of them -- hold that what the individuality creates for itself upon incarnation is only a minimal personality, and then as the body grows and matures, that minimal personality develops into a good number of fairly distinct personalities, each suited for this or that particular situation in life. In most of us, most of those personalities work very well together as we move through life, though often there will be a few of them that need to be squelched or i"mprisoned" in a sort of internal dungeon. (Think of how variously a person presents themself in their interactions with parents, or with work colleagues, or wilh their children, or with a spouse or lover, or with a total stranger, etc. etc.) With very long-standing relations, as for example in a rewarding very long-term marriage, the two or more parties also slowly may develop a joint personality (which exists alongside of their individual sets of personalities). -- Only rarely do the multiple personalities fail to work well together; then you get a psychological disorder (Multiple Personality Disorder), but this is an outlying and uncommon phenomenon.

This teaching about "multiple selves," to the best of my knowledge, was independently developed by Charles Godfrey Leland, the folklorist, and by Frederic W. H. Myers, one of the founders of the Society for Psychical Research, and picked up by William James, the psychologist who wrote The Varieties of Religious Experience. During the last century, Fritz Leiber was one of the SF writers who held fast to this theory. There are a few academics now who are deepening the study of what one of them (Ann Taves) has called the "frangible self" -- another excellent term for what I have been calling "multiple selves."

As for séances, I think the Theosophists are right that what speaks or acts through the medium is in most instances not really the person who it claims to be. And of course there is a whole unpublicized "insiders' literature" of how to earn your living as a fraudulent medium, too.

replies.title: Re: Communicating with the Departed
replies.content: That's one of the reasons I specified the traditions I follow, rather than occultism in general; occultists are an independent-minded bunch and no two of them agree about everything!  I sometimes wonder whether the distinction between my model and yours -- whether one sees the personality as a flexible, somewhat polymorphous thing or a collection of separate things -- is a matter of emphasis; does one stress the common features of the various expressions, or the differences between them?

replies.title: Re: Communicating with the Departed
replies.content:
I think it's mostly a difference in emphasis, and in how your culture is inclined to talk about such things -- and also of what feels intuitively right to the individual occultist. Leland came to his model through thinking about how alien and uncongenial to his waking self was the self that shaped his dreams: "If the two of us belonged to the same club, we should never speak." Later on he concluded, again mostly from introspection, that a man will commonly contain at least one self that is female, and a woman at least one self that is male, among their respecttive multiplicities of selves.

Fritz Leiber's preference for a theory of multiple selves, for a contrasting example, likely goes back to his early childhood as the son of two actors and as a actor himself in his 'teens. Among the students in my courses on magic at Brown, one oif the earliest and best of them was also the child of actors and an actor herself. The "multiple selves" theory resonated deeply with her, and felt intuitively more true than the theory of a somewhat polymorphous self or personality. She knew from experience what it was like to so deeply lose herself in a role that it was quite hard to return to her everyday self offstage. (She said this "returning" was a skill that every actor had to learn eventually, and that the learning of it was hard. She hadn't quite got the hang of returning even by the time she graduated, though she clearly has acquired it by now.)

I never was an actor, but I also felt that the "multiple selves" model was more true of (for?) me than the somewhat flexible polymorphous personality model. But it's probably a largely subjective thing. One thing I have done with it is that I have made for myself a magician-self, distinct from my other selves.

replies.title: Re: Communicating with the Departed
replies.content: You probably know already that W.E. Butler strongly advised the creation of a magician-self -- he had a ring, and when he put on the ring he took on his magician-self. That's not something Dion Fortune taught, as far as I know, so he may have gotten it from his other teacher, the Theosophist and Liberal Catholic Chrch bishop Robert King.

replies.title: Re: Communicating with the Departed
replies.content: I did not know (or perhaps remember) that, though I may have read it in one or another of his books (most of which I have).  Curiously enough, I too have used a special ring -- a silver signet ring with a maltese cross, made for me as a birthday present when I turned (IIRC) 8, though I don't always do so.  When I do use it, it goes on the ring finger of my right hand, where it balances the gold wedding ring on the ring finger of my left hand.  Then I say, "I stand with the sun on my right hand and the moon on my left," followed by some statement of my purpose.  That way the two luminaries are opposite to their metals in the rings: this contradiction seems to help me enter "the time that is no time, the place that is no place."

replies.title: Re: Communicating with the Departed
replies.content:
Dion Fortune taught something very similar to the magician-self -- she called it the 'Horvendile body' associated with a 'magical name'. She discusses it in the section of _Applied Magic_ titled "A Magical Body."

Gray Hat

Now that I've accepted reincarnation, my past-life memories seem to be surfacing more easily, but it's gradual. As I've said before, the earliest memory I'm sure of (which feels like centuries ago, latter medieval era I'm guessing) contains knowledge I didn't have, and everything else checked out. I also mentioned dying on the Titanic, and I realized that I've always remembered what it's like to fill up your lungs with cold ocean water, which is something I've never experienced in this life. It was always there in the back of my mind, and I didn't question the elephant in the room until now. Also, I remember it being quite dark, with lights on in the ship, maybe decorations? Not sure, but I checked, and the Titanic did sink in the middle of the night. I wasn't clear on that, but it always lines up!

Any further pointers on past-life recollection would be appreciated -- so far, I've realized that it has to come naturally, and they emerge very differently than my own imaginings. Also, could one's interests & fixations be tied to a past life? I think my obsession with WWII comes from one in which I died in it as a soldier (although I could've been Soviet or Axis for all I know). I'm also a bit of a weeb, and for some reason, I've always been drawn to the Edo period of Japan in particular (early modern period), rather than the seemingly more interesting feudal wars before that. I wonder if another past life was then and there, under the Tokugawa Shogunate, although this is purely guesswork.

Anyway, it's an immense relief to know that this is just the latest incarnation in a long series of lives, and I'm allowed to make mistakes and learn along my winding spiritual path. For atheist materialists, death is a yawning void of nothingness, a sort of Lovecraftian horror waiting for them, casting a shadow of nihilism over their lives. And then for many Abrahamists, there is the stark judgment of heaven or hell, eternal bliss or torment, with enormous pressure to "get it right" in a single life (at least my native Catholicism posits Limbo and Purgatory).
These common perceptions of the afterlife must bring about alot of neurosis and pathology, and contribute to the malaise I sense in the modern West. Perhaps a wider understanding of reincarnation, and how it's influenced by accumulated karma, would be beneficial?

Regards, xcalibur/djs

The one thing I know is the thing you've already noted, which is that they're best left to rise by themselves.

In her family magic was for women only, and not meant for men to know, or even know about. (And she had no daughters to pass it on to, either.)

That line of women's magic goes back only as far as my great-great-grandmother, and was cobbled together by those four generations of women in Joliet (Illinois) and the San Francisco Bay area out of various parts of Spiritualism, New Thought, Christian Science, Ella Wheeler Wilcox, Nellie Beighle (with her "electric arm"), Max Muehlenbruch (the "Prophet of Alameda"), and maybe a bit from the Swedenborgian Church of the New Jerusalem. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Hello Dermotok,
I took up swimming myself at the end of 2022. While I don’t have any magical tips to help you, one piece of advice that was tremendously helpful for me was “swim around your breathing, don’t breathe around your swimming.” Focus first on establishing a good breathing rhythm, and let that rhythm dictate your stroke rate and your timing for everything else. It really all comes back to the breath. If you aren’t inhaling and exhaling completely and rhythmically, you won’t be able to swim for long or swim well. As far as unwanted thoughts intruding into your mind (for me, swimming in the Gulf of Mexico, its always sharks), once again, recognize where your mind is at, and redirect it back to your breathing. This process always reminds me of discursive meditation. And of course there are many powers greater than us that can be invoked for protection and safe passage before entering the water, most spiritual traditions have one or more. As a peripheral Catholic I find the legend of Saint Adjutor of Vernon quite inspiring. Hope that this helps you on your swimming journey. Taking up regular ocean swimming at 37 has been utterly transformative for me, it is absolutely worth the effort to learn how to swim well.
-Stephen

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Dermotok,

I highly recommend you find a good swim instructor. If you can't do that, videos. Never swim alone just in case there is an issue. (Heaven forbid!)

Barring that and without knowing the exact stroke you are working or actually seeing you swim (get a swim instructor):

First thing you need to do is RELAX!. Seriously. Keep in mind that the body moves differently through the water than it does on land. (Duh! I know) You can't force movement in the same way, you really do need to "flow" with the water. "Slow is smooth and smooth is fast" in this situation. (In many situations, but this one in particular)

Try floating in the water face up first. You need to push your hips up to the surface and breath shallow keeping a bit of air in your lungs for flotation. This will help you relax. If you struggle you will sink. RELAX!

Second thing is to stay in shallow water if possible. If you run into issues, stand up! Take a few breaths to calm yourself, and then continue.

Break each part of the stroke you are learning into parts.
-Legs first. Get a kick board, or borrow one and only kick. For the basic kick you need to keep your legs fairly straight. In fact, just concentrate on keeping them straight. You are using your hip muscles for this. (Yes, you will use others, but concentrate there) Then start kicking. Kick again and then kick some more and then keep kicking. Kick until you hate kicking and keep going.(Did I mention you should kick) Lay on your back for dry land kicking.

Try "dry land" swimming to learn the separate parts. This means that you lay on the pool deck and practice the parts of the stroke there first. No chance of sinking or running out of air. You may look strange, but if you're serious, you won't care.

-Arms next. Depending on the stroke, you will practice breath with this. Start out breathing every stroke. Once you are comfortable and compitent you could try every other stroke. This will take some time to build up to. Don't rush it! Do not kick! You will need to concentrate on keeping your hips high in the water.

-Then put it all together.

THEN get in the water, and work the same process there.

After you are done with your physical practice, meditation is next. Lay on the pool deck on your back.(No sleeping!) Imagine yourself in the pool swimming with a perfect stroke for 5 minutes or so. Visualization helps more than most realize. This is part of the "Perfect practice makes perfect" mantra.

RELAX!

Just for a data point, I learned 4 different strokes in 4 weeks this way...with a great coach and about 2 hours in the pool 5 days a week. It will take you longer, and there is no way I could possibly share every detail that makes a positive impact.

If you are serious, you really should have someone teach you. They would be able to help immensely.

Regards,
Bert

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Dermotok,

As a side, I hope that didn't come across harshly. I realize you can't see my face or hear the inflection of my voice.

I think it's fantastic that you are learning to swim. I only wanted to offer advice that helped me become very comfortable in the water. Learn to separate and then combine the movements so they became muscle-memory in a very safe environment. Then, once in the water, you can focus on enjoying the swim instead of struggling to keep everything straight in your head.

Technique is far more important than power initially. Power will come once your nervous system has the patterns in place.

Focus on one thing at a time and take it slow. Swimming only looks easy. It becomes graceful and smooth only after proper technique and a lot of time in the water.

Remember that you spent 9 months in fluid before you came into this world. It is a part of you. Don't fear it unnecessarily.

Regards,
Bert

replies.title: starting easily
replies.content:
I was fortunate to have excellent swim lessons as a child. One of the things my parents got right. Good for you for taking up what can be a very enjoyable lifelong pasttime, workout, social fun, and/or flowing meditation, as you choose!

I agree that if it all possible, tutoring or an adult beginner class can help.

Are you starting with easier methods first?
1 Float on back with arms outstretched, so no need to manage breathing.
2 Survival stance: Pull up knees, put face down, look up as needed for a breath but no need to move yourself around as you bob in place.
3 Lie face down on a foam body board or inflated air mattress. Practice dogpaddle and basic kicks, while head is fully out of the water.
4 Breast stroke, and side stroke ("reach up to pluck the apple from the tree and put it in the basket") match arm motion with time for a breath, in a way that crawl stroke doesn't do automatically.
5 Add wrist and ankle encircling floats with basic crawl practice, so buoyancy is assured as you practice the initially tricky four way combination of turning head, breathing, having arm windmill going, and meanwhile continue flutter kick nonstop. It IS a lot to learn at once!

Baby steps, the aquatic equivalent of crawl then walk then run. Except crawl stroke is more like equivalent of running.

Do you have goggles, hair out of eyes management etc in place? Maybe there is physical distraction that's a valid claim on your attention. A competent instructor would help spot this kind of issue.

Christopher from California

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You can expect to gain at least three things by an extended course of discursive meditation on the Tree of Life. The first is a benefit of every kind of meditation: it decreases stress and brings about a cascade of quantifiable health benefits. (This has been shown in dozens of controlled double-blinded studies.) The second is a benefit of discursive meditation: it makes your mind clearer and teaches you how to think in a more creative, productive, and useful way. The third is a benefit specific to the Tree of Life: the Tree is a map of the deep structure of consciousness, and knowing your way around it is like being given a good accurate map of a city you don't know well. You can find your way around and get to places you want to go!

I also, however, got the distinct impression that I missed something, and that no one is praying to me. Other than that, I haven't gotten any clear answers as to what is drawing his attention.

I am unable to say what it is at this point that has drawn his attention to me; but as the synchronicities around the god have continued to be positive, I am inclined to make sure I thank him for his blessings, but otherwise wait until he decides to make it clear why he's taken an interest in me.

Do you have any advice for this situation beyond what I am doing?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
When I was living in Indonesia, I found the use in Bahasa Indonesia of the proper name Allah as a generic term for divinity to be somewhat uncomfortable. At a prayer circle of Catholics reciting Hail Mary's, I was surprised to be chanting "Holy Mary, mother of Allah". Obviously, I had to get over my discomfort, as that is just the word for god in Bahasa. In English we don't do that, so it would seem weird to us to refer to all the Yahwehs in the Hindu pantheon or all the Buddahs in Santeria.

Anyway, I had no more problem playing gamelan for Allah's pleasure on the Muslim high holy days than I did playing for Siva or Ganesha's pleasure on Hindu high holy days. Allah is a great god, as his followers love to say. He will bless you for your blessings, so enjoy his interest in you.

— Cristophe
